nativeRange.habitat: Found in streams, rivers, and marshes within tropical rainforest understory, where it typically grows anchored to submerged wood, roots, and rocks at the water's edge. Plants are often partially or fully emersed in their natural environment.

decorPreferences: shaded areas to reduce leaf algae

goodWith: African cichlids (leaves are tough enough to resist most nibbling)

summary: Anubias is a slow-growing West African rhizome plant with tough, waxy leaves, broadly regarded as one of the most forgiving plants for beginners. Its only firm requirement is that the rhizome must be attached to hardscape and never buried — burial causes rot.
careNotes:
Anubias barteri is a slow-growing epiphytic rhizome plant native to streams and rainforest margins across West Africa. It tolerates a wide range of water conditions and is broadly regarded as beginner-friendly, but carries one critical requirement: the rhizome — the thick horizontal stem from which leaves and roots emerge — MUST NOT BE BURIED in substrate. Burial causes the rhizome to rot, typically resulting in the collapse of the plant. Instead, attach the specimen to driftwood, rocks, or other hardscape using thread, fishing line, or cyanoacrylate (super) glue; the roots will anchor on their own over time.

The leaves are tough and waxy, and most herbivorous fish — including the majority of African cichlids — tend to leave them undamaged, though goldfish and large plant-eaters may still cause harm. Because Anubias grows slowly, leaves are prone to algae accumulation; placing the plant in shaded areas of the tank, under taller plants, behind hardscape, or in lower-light zones, substantially reduces algae build-up on leaf surfaces.

Lighting: low-to-medium light is preferred and produces vibrant growth with minimal algae; higher light accelerates growth but increases algae risk correspondingly.

CO2 injection is not required but is reported to improve growth rate and leaf quality. Regular liquid fertilisation is beneficial; root tabs are less critical, as most nutrient uptake occurs via the rhizome and roots in the water column rather than through substrate. Sudden parameter swings can trigger melt and should be avoided.

Anubias barteri var. barteri is the broad-leaf form widely encountered in the trade under the names 'Anubias Barteri' or simply 'Anubias', though var. nana is generally considered the more popular variety in the hobby owing to its smaller size and versatility across tank setups. Numerous other varieties and cultivars are also commonly available — including 'Coffeefolia', 'Petite', var. caladiifolia, var. glabra, 'Broad Leaf', 'Round / Golden Coin', 'Butterfly', 'Striped', and 'Dark Angel' — most sharing identical care requirements; the principal differences are leaf shape and adult size (var. nana remains considerably smaller at 5–10 cm).
breedingNotes: Anubias barteri is propagated by rhizome division: using a sharp blade, cut a healthy rhizome into sections, each retaining at least 3–4 healthy leaves and some attached roots. Each section should be re-attached to hardscape rather than buried. The species can also flower underwater, producing a small white spathe-and-spadix inflorescence characteristic of the Araceae family, though seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source page for Anubias barteri var. barteri. Confirmed origin (West Africa), height (25–45 cm), low light demand, low CO2 demand, slow growth, easy difficulty, and the critical rhizome-must-not-be-buried instruction.

additional.name: Buce Plant
